data.title: Dell's 8% FCF Yield Is Not A Value Trap (Q2 2026 Earnings Review)
data.description:
Dell Q2 2026 Earnings Review

Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) reported earnings yesterday, and we now have a clearer picture of the AI infrastructure build-out process. In January 2024, I considered Dell one of the top three picks for the year, and I was rewarded as the stock soared. By the end of the year, however, I changed my rating and moved to a neutral stance as I was seeing massive growth in the AI server business, although margin dilution was becoming disproportionate. The PC business, on the other hand, while highly profitable, showed some softness as the corporate replacement cycle wasn't playing out at the speed that was first forecasted. In short, lingering operational uncertainties made me more cautious, but I surely didn't lose interest in one of the key companies to monitor to understand the impact of AI. In my Q1 earnings preview, I considered the quarter pivotal because it would determine whether the AI server backlog growth would also go hand in hand with stronger PC sales. As a matter of fact, Q1 proved to be the turning point.

Today, we are going to go through Dell's Q2 earnings report, and we will see record results both in AI server and PC sales.

Dell Q2 Main Financials

Dell beat revenue and EPS estimates, reporting $29.78B in sales ($590M above the consensus) and EPS of $2.32 ($0.03 above the consensus). It also upped its Q3 guidance by almost $700M, with a midpoint revenue forecast of $27B. For the fiscal year, Dell now expects revenues of $107B, which is a $2.16B increase versus the prior guidance. EPS should be $9.55, which is $0.18 above what was previously announced. Considering that the stock dropped to $125 after earnings, we have a forward P/E of 13.

Dell's revenues increased 19% YoY, and its operating income of $1.8B was up 27% YoY. Operating cash flow generation was robust at $2.5B. What fueled these results was the shipment of $10B of AI solutions in the first six months of the year, which is already above the FY25 result. The Servers and Networking business grew 69% YoY in this quarter alone, achieving $12.9B in revenues, finally helping Dell capitalize on the massive order backlog it had built over the past few quarters. Consider that the long-term value proposition of Dell is to grow the CSG business at 2-3% per year and the ISG business at a 6-8% pace.

We also see that, in Q2, Dell's ISG division surpassed CSG, which means that Dell's revenues have now, as their main driver, AI server demand rather than PC sales.

Client Solutions Group is doing ok, reporting revenues up 1%, with commercial revenues of $10.8B being 2% above last year's. However, there are reasons to be bullish because AI PCs are projected to represent 31% of the total PC market by the end of this year (2024: 15.6%). So, we can expect a PC replacement cycle to pick up speed in Q3 and Q4 as the market is being reshaped by AI, where Dell is steadily gaining market share (28% in mainstream server share, right now). Volume now means service and maintenance later.

Nonetheless, the stock is trading down, and it's hard to understand why. A reason could be seen in Dell's margins. Although Dell's ISG revenues are exploding, we still have an operating margin of 8.8%, down from 11% in Q2 2025. So, the continued margin deterioration and compression are causing investors to value Dell's surging sales with lower multiples. We must, however, acknowledge that operating expense was down 4% YoY, amounting to only 11% of total revenues, showing that Dell is focusing on cost management to address inefficiencies. An analyst asked about AI server margins, trying to grasp when they will improve and by what order of magnitude. The answer was not extremely convincing. We heard that AI is gross dollar accretive but rate dilutive, which means that AI servers have lower margins. Of course, Dell talked about value engineering, which will optimize production costs, scale effect, and higher enterprise penetration. The latter, however, means that through AI server sales, Dell aims at selling other products and services such as storage, networking, and similar. So, it appears that hardware sales are right now the Trojan horse to capture customers that can then be converted to services. Moreover, Dell did admit that it was particularly aggressive in seizing important deals. This means that competition is part of the equation and that we would make a big mistake considering Dell an undisputed AI hardware leader. In Q3, we know that traditional servers and Dell IP storage will weigh more on the sales mix, and this should lead to higher profitability margins.

Dell is clearly growing above its long-term goal. Below, we see how the backlog exploded in Q1 and is still strong in Q2, despite impressive shipments of $8.2B and orders worth $5.6B. Moreover, Dell states that its "pipeline remains multiples of the backlog", meaning that future demand is far larger than the orders that have already been placed.

Now, the question is whether this impressive growth is sustainable or not. Well, during the earnings call, we learned that "over 70% of the installed base is running on 14th generation servers or older". Right now, AI demand is for 16th and 17th-generation servers. This means that the replacement cycle is still in its earlier innings.

On the PC side, Windows 10 support ends this October. Around half of Dell's installed base still runs on Windows 10, and this pushes for a replacement cycle that will probably extend through 2026. It is a great catalyst for AI PC sales, which should make Dell's bottom line fatter.

DELL stock valuation

Record shipments and revenues were the highlights of the quarter, but the real tale is margin. Profitability matters more at this point because we have already digested the unleashing of AI, but we still don't have a clear view of its profitability. Our checklist for the next few quarters will then monitor gross margins and their trajectory, the backlog burn compared to new orders, the AI PC refresh cycle, and how Dell's services and networking businesses grow.

Right now, I think the market is punishing Dell too heavily for its report, which actually seems to be a solid foundation for prosperous years ahead. In a certain way, the market is pricing Dell as a commodity provider, considering its hardware as an easily replaceable product. Nonetheless, Dell is acquiring expertise in supplying and maintaining data centers that can set it apart from the rest. Right now, I think it is key that Dell increases its market share, as from these gains, higher-margin revenue will descend in the form of services.

Dell currently trades at a forward P/E of 13 and a forward PEG ratio of around 0.9. Considering its steady and growing cash flows, we have a forward FCF yield above 8%. Usually, this happens when we have a value trap. But I don't see Dell as one because of its booming AI business. Therefore, I am upgrading my rating to a buy and consider this sell-off as an interesting entry point.

data.title: Nathan's Famous: Tread Carefully, Even As Takeover Potential Remains
data.description:
Back in April, I wrote a bullish write-up on Nathan's Famous (NASDAQ:NATH), the famed purveyor of hot dogs that in recent years has become primarily a brand licensing company. In my initial write-up, I focused on the main catalyst for NATH stock.

That would be the company's expected sale, either to its main licensee, or to private equity buyers specializing in the ownership of restaurant brands. Shares are up since the spring, but as many may recall, NATH took a big dive back in July, on zero news.

While not for certain, this may have been due to a potential sale falling through. Irrespective of the root cause, shares have started to bounce back. Is this a sign to buy/add to a position?

Not so fast. While I have discovered that there may be more than one natural buyer for this business, comparing the stock’s current valuation to a potential deal price, and taking into account recent quarterly results, it may be best to wait for additional weakness.

Nathan's Famous: Recap and Recent Developments

As discussed previously, over the past two decades, Nathan's Famous has transformed from being the trademark owner/operator of a smattering of also-ran fast food restaurant concepts, to focusing entirely on its namesake brand.

While this has not necessarily translated into explosive growth in terms of Nathan's Famous locations, this transformation has turned the company into a high-margin, consistently-profitable enterprise.

That is, NATH's main income stream stems not from its franchise network, nor from its "Branded Menu Program," through which foodservice companies sell Nathan's Famous-branded products at venues such as airports and casinos.

Rather, the company's main "business" is through the licensing of its brand to Smithfield Foods (SFD), which manufactures and sells Nathan's Famous-branded hot dog and sausage products through its John Morrell Food Group unit.

Alongside the deal with Smithfield, Nathan's Famous also has licensing agreements with other food companies, for the manufacture of Nathan's Famous-branded french fires, hors d'oeuvres, mustard, and pickles. The company even has a licensing deal for its proprietary spices used to give the sausage products their distinct "Nathan's Famous" flavor.

During NATH's fiscal year ending March 30, 2025, license royalties produced $37.4 million in revenue. This represented a little over a quarter of the company's overall sales. However, given the high-margin nature of this royalty income, this segment is the company's primary profit center, especially as FY2025 operating income came in at $36.5 million.

Not only has licensing become Nathan's Famous' main profit center: this revenue continues to grow at a steady clip, with licensing revenue increasing by around 11.4% during the prior fiscal year.

With this, it's no surprise that, earlier this year, investors took the rumors seriously that the company was putting itself up for sale. Not only did Nathan's Famous' Executive Chairman, Howard Lorber, appear to be itching to sell, given the headline-making divestiture of some of his other publicly-traded holdings, including Douglas Elliman (DOUG).

Given NATH's steady licensing income stream, plus the potential for growth/cost synergies if Nathan's were to be combined with another fast food company, it seemed like a natural target for private equity buyers.

During the first half of the year, especially in the months following publication of my initial NATH write-up, investors bought even more so into the narrative that the company was on the verge of getting acquired.

Again though, with the stock's sudden drop on zero news back in July, hope and hype about a forthcoming sale of Nathan's took a serious breather. On August 8, Nathan's Famous released Q1 FY2026 results. While not horrendous per se, certain key numbers may suggest more challenging times in the coming quarters.

For the quarter ending June 30, 2025, NATH reported roughly $47 million in revenue, around a 5% increase compared to the prior year's quarter. However, operating income fell by roughly 7%, from $13.7 million to $12.8 million. Net income fell by 3.8%, while EPS of $2.16 came in 4.9% below reported EPS for Q1 FY2025.

A big reason for this was a decline in license royalties, from $12.9 million during Q1 FY2025 to $12.4 million during Q1 FY2026, a decrease of around 3.9%. In the 10-Q, management noted that license revenue went down due to a 15% decrease in retail volume that was only partially offset by a 12% increase in selling prices.

While Branded Product Program sales increased 11% year-over-year, volumes were flat, signaling that this revenue increase represented just the passing along of higher beef prices to the consumer.

In short, while the latest numbers could be viewed as acceptable or satisfactory, it is concerning that inflation, not to mention other possible factors like increased economic uncertainty and decreasing traffic to foodservice venues featuring Nathan's Famous products, are having a negative impact on overall results.

The Latest on Valuation

In my last NATH stock article, I valued the company at around $125 per share. This valuation estimate was based upon the potential for an acquirer to buy the company, at a price in the upper range of fast food company valuations.

That is, while NATH traded at an EV/EBITDA ratio in the low-teens, a multiple slightly above that of other fast food companies like Jack in the Box (JACK) and The Wendy's Company (WEN), I argued that a buyer would perhaps be willing to buy NATH, at a deal multiple in the 14x-15x EBITDA range.

The reasons for this were numerous. First, a higher valuation was justified due to the steady cash flows generated by the licensing business. Second, in order to gain greater control of this brand, licensee Smithfield may be incentivized to acquire the company, even at a premium price.

Third, and most importantly, the high potential to materially improve NATH's profitability, simply from the elimination of redundant overhead. Irrespective of whether a strategic buyer acquires the company, consolidating overhead into its own operators, or a financial buyer, like Roark Capital, bought it/combined it with its own restaurant brand company, Inspire Brands, this cost savings potential would help to drive a higher sale price.

With the release of the latest fiscal data from Nathan's, let's take a second look at valuation. Currently, NATH has a market cap of around $441 million. Add $54.9 million in debt/lease liabilities, and subtract the company's $26.9 million cash position, and we get an enterprise value of around $469 million.

As of June 30, 2025, trailing twelve month (TTM) EBITDA stood at $36.5 million. Hence, the stock currently trades at TTM EV/EBITDA ratio of around 12.8. Admittedly, it may make more sense to value Nathan's Famous at an EBITDA multiple in the low-teens rather than the high-teens.

After all, not only do fast food companies like JACK and WEN trade at similar multiples. Let's consider some of the more brand-focused foods companies as well. In fact, there may be a lesser-known publicly-traded company out there that could arguably be considered NATH's closest public peer.

I'm talking about J&J Snack Foods (JJSF). Like Nathan's Famous, J&J's portfolio is focused largely on snack food products commonly purchased at venues. J&J's brands include SUPERPRETZEL, Icee, Dippin Dots, and Funnel Cake Factory. JJSF stock is currently valued at a similar TTM EV/EBITDA ratio as NATH: around 12.8.

However, an acquirer like J&J may be willing to acquire NATH, at a valuation on par with the forecasted deal price discussed in my initial writeup, $120 to $125 per share. At this valuation range, Nathan's Famous' enterprise value comes out to between $518.8 million and $539.2 million, which would mean a valuation of between 14.2 and 14.8 times EBITDA.

That may represent a premium to JJSF’s own valuation, but this could be more than made up for by potential cost and growth synergies.

Why J&J Snack Foods is Another Natural Buyer

The jury's still out on whether Nathan's Famous' reported "go shop" efforts will result in a sale of the company. The aforementioned price action may suggest that a potential deal failed to work out, but that's not entirely certain. That said, it's important to note that Smithfield isn't the only natural buyer for this business.

Again, this could be a great "bolt on" acquisition opportunity for Roark Capital's Inspire Brands unit, or for one of the other fast food companies that own multiple brands.

However, digging deeper into J&J Snack Foods, I am even more convinced that a transaction between these two companies could create a great scenario, for both NATH stock investors, as well as investors in JJSF stock. Yes, while NATH and JJSF are very similar, JJSF is different in that it is not just a licensor of snack food brands. J&J Snack Foods is also involved in food manufacturing.

Still, that may make the company an even stronger suitor for Nathan's Famous. Over time, J&J could either end licensing agreements, bringing certain products, such as the frozen food products, in-house. Or, perhaps a purchase of Nathan's could be a springboard for J&J to pursue its own asset-light transformation.

That's not all. There may be considerable cross-selling opportunities between the product portfolios of both companies. Nathan's Famous locations would make great outlets for SUPERPRETZELS and Icee products. Product-related cost/growth synergies would likely take years to take shape.

However, as is the case with other potential buyers of NATH, JJSF could right off the bat integrate Nathan's Famous' corporate overhead into its own. Last fiscal year, NATH had G&A expenses totaling $14.5 million.

While it is entirely speculative at this whether JJSF could be a potential bidder for NATH, don’t be surprised if it ends up happening.

Downside Risk Greater Than It May Seem

With the NATH stock price currently at around $106 per share, shares may at first appear to be a worthwhile special situation. After all, with NATH rumored to be pursuing a sale since early this year, it could potentially be a matter of months before an offer in the $120 to $125 per share range, or 13.2% to 17.9% above current prices, suddenly emerges.

However, what happens if a deal fails to materialize? As noted in my initial writeup, NATH has a limited amount of outstanding float, and has relatively low daily trading volume. If it becomes more clear that a sale of the company is not happening, shares will undoubtedly come under pressure, as those in the stock solely as a takeover play make their exit.

Admittedly, perhaps not back to where shares traded prior to the emergence of the takeover target catalyst, as I noted previously. NATH’s fair valuation on an EV/EBITDA basis may limit how far shares pull back from present price levels.

Still, while a fast return to prior lows may not occur if there’s no longer a takeover catalyst, shares could continue to pull back, if the negative growth seen in NATH’s latest quarterly results persists in the coming quarters.

Although NATH may trade at a similar valuation to other fast food stocks on an EV/EBITDA basis, it should be noted that it trades at a premium valuation, in terms of price-to-earnings (P/E).

NATH’s TTM P/E ratio is currently 18.7x. Compare that to the valuation of other restaurant stocks, most of which sport P/E ratios in the low-to-mid teens. A combination of declining earnings and multiple compression could result in a far larger move lower for Nathan’s Famous than you may currently expect.

Bottom Line on NATH Stock

If you bought into NATH at prices under $100 per share, it may be worthwhile to hold onto a position. Relative to your cost basis, the potential upside may make the downside risk worthwhile.

However, at $106 per share, the risk/reward proposition is starting to become less favorable. Although there may be numerous synergies that strategic and financial buyers could extract with NATH stock already at a fair value, potential bidders may not offer much in terms of a takeover premium.

Alternatively, if Nathan’s Famous decides to hold out for a higher offer, in the meantime shares could come under pressure, due to a combination of frustration with a lack of deal progress, plus the potential for subsequent quarterly results to show disappointing metrics like declining sales and profitability.

data.title: Short-Term Energy Outlook, August 2025
data.description:
A guest post by D C

The EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) was published recently. A summary in chart form.

Note the drop in Brent prices compared to the July STEO.

The chart above shows the relationship between stock builds or draws with changes in the price of oil in real (inflation-adjusted) dollars. When there is a stock build, prices decrease, and a stock draw leads to a price increase. The EIA expects oil stocks to build and oil prices are expected to fall.

Increased output from OPEC+, as shown above, is the reason for the expected oil stock build.

WTI is expected to average $48/b in 2026, oil rigs will go to the barn at that price.

Gasoline prices are expected to fall.

Diesel prices fall, but by less than gasoline due to higher refinery margins for diesel fuel in 2025 and 2026.

Very low distillate fuel stocks (below the 5-year range) led to the higher refinery margins for distillate fuel.

Natural gas prices are expected to rise due to high demand for natural gas exported as LNG.

Natural gas output is relatively flat in 2025 and 2026 despite the high natural gas prices expected due to lower associated gas from tight oil plays as tight oil output declines due to low oil prices.

High demand from data centers and AI leads to a sharp rise in commercial electricity use, reaching the level of residential electricity consumption for the first time since 2001 (earliest date I have data for).

Coal output falls as coal power plants continue to close and plant stockpiles of coal are used rather than new production starting in 2025H2.

Coal exports are also expected to fall due to Chinese tariffs on US coal.

Note the World liquids stock build in 2025 and 2026.

US shale gas proved reserves were 393 TCF at the end of 2023, and cumulative shale gas wet output was 302 TCF from Jan. 2000 to Dec. 2023.

Exports of natural gas are expected to increase by 4.6 BCF/d from 2024 to 2026 and are likely to increase more in 2027 and 2028 as new LNG trains become operational in Louisiana and Texas.

The medium scenario above is my best guess for US shale gas, with the low and high scenarios based on both USGS assessments of the F95 and F5 TRR for shale plays and the analysis of Patzek et al. on various shale gas plays. Peak is in 2028 at 97.85 BCF/d (8.8 BCF/d higher than 2024). I expect there is a 90% probability that output will fall between the high and low scenarios, with an equal chance that output will be higher or lower than the medium scenario.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Revisiting My Lowered Maersk Target After Q2 2025
data.description:
In my last article on A.P. Møller - Mærsk A/S (OTCPK:AMKBY, MAERSK-B.CO), I lowered my price target to account for what I expected and viewed to be an elevated tariff risk that would impact the company negatively. Since that that article, found here, we've had Q2 2025 results, which we'll be looking at here. However, as part of these results, the company has raised its full-year guidance, and the share price and valuation have followed suit.

The question, therefore, becomes, was my target cut in my last article a mistake, or is this a case of exuberance on the part of the market and not representative of the longer-term thesis for the company.

Investing in shipping long-term is tricky - it's why I view the sector as a mostly in-and-out sort of segment, targeting low prices and selling the investment when the company goes up again.

it bears mentioning that the results of the company were, in fact, negative from an EBIT perspective - and despite this, the share price has risen significantly since, with a double-digit RoR in a relatively short time.

So what is causing the company to be more positive here? Maersk is primarily a play on volume trends and freight pricing - and while the company can never really be argued to be "unsafe," it can certainly be said to, over time, be a poor investment if you buy it at an inflated valuation. Those who bought shares over 25,000 DKK for the native are no doubt regretting that action, and I don't think we'll see the company moving up here for some time.

A.P. Möller Maersk - Upside for the long term might be possible, but at this valuation, the return is more uncertain

The biggest piece of news this quarter was the company's assumption for increased container volume growth - it's likely also this that has facilitated the increase in valuation. While outperforming, it's of course not a massive one.

But an outperformance is still an outperformance - note, however, that my average position cost basis is now up more than 15%, and as such, I would view my overall thesis as successful.

EBIT for the company fell to $845M during the quarter, which was at the level I expected it to be. Maersk's strong execution is not a surprise to me - and therefore also not an argument to increase the company's valuation by itself. The positives that I would instead point to for Maersk, which I didn't necessarily expect, was a year-on-year higher margin level, a sequential increase here.

Terminals furthermore showed better results, due to improved volume and revenues.

But let's head to the essence of the matter - container expected growth in terms of volume. The company now expects the market to grow by 2-4% as opposed to the previous expectation of negative growth, entirely due to the company's focus on international trade - meaning the increase in volume is expected to come from non-U.S. trading segments and flows. And this is, by the way, with the Red Sea disruption still considered as a major component of trading flows until the very end of this year.

So what can Maersk influence?

Things like its own operational efficiencies, for one.

Since the company has been executing strongly on this for the past few quarters, I don't expect this to change significantly over the next few quarters either. But it's important to note at the same time that Maersk does not control global pricing or flows. It's a company that is forced to "go along" with global trade trends. This is why the segments like services, logistics, and terminals are so crucial, and why so many larger shipping companies are focusing on them - they are segments and revenue flows that remain far less affected by global trade.

At the same time, the companies involved try to use strategies like the Gemini model, which Maersk is part of, to improve the volatility and cyclicality of this segment. Dealing with the "hills and valleys" found here is crucial for the businesses involved.

This model/cooperation with Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) is still less than 10 months old at the time of writing this article, but I believe it to be one of the key components to not only "decarbonize," one of the main attractions, but on reliability and profit.

Any fair value or price target increase on the basis of Q2 '25 should, in this analyst's opinion, be very minimal. Why?

Because while it may be true that we have increased visibility for potential profit this year, thanks to a greater clarity on tariffs, I believe it would be wrong to consider the tariff situation with NA to be "solved" here. And for a shipping business like Maersk, any volatility in these trade deals/tariffs (which I would argue has been proven to happen at this point) would result in volatility in earnings. I will also argue that the eventual outcome of China/U.S. tariff negotiations will play a big role in the valuation of the company, and while things here seem more positive than they did 3 months ago, I would also argue that it's far from done. Chinese business dealings are currently quite "on hold," and how this goes will decide whether Maersk finishes at the higher end or at the lower end of even its updated guidance.

Add this uncertainty to the already-existing slew of arguments or cautions against a too-high Maersk valuation, and you may begin to see why I lowered my target in the first place. I'm talking about the fact that Maersk has been unable to expand logistics without sacrificing margins, or at least without raising them - and Gemini is replicable, with other companies already looking into similar hub-and-spoke models. It's simply the next step in world shipping efficiency, and the company does not have a sole "right" to this.

While the segment of global shipping has become more and more specialized over the past decade and more, and is now mostly excluding any smaller players or entrants, there is enough existing competition to Maersk to prevent the company from going significantly higher without an underlying sort of catalyst, such as the freight pricing increase a couple of years ago.

You'd be hard-pressed, in my opinion, to argue for a specific "moat" for this business as well. I do not see one, or a reason for one. Even if the 14% of global capacity for ocean shipping is impressive, the number of competitors on the global scale makes it clear that this is in no way an easy segment to operate, even with almost 15% of the capacity.

What Maersk is doing is ensuring a high rate of contracted customers, which again raises the issue of its ability to forecast, as well as using models like Gemini to improve where it can. It's part of what is driving efficiency and strategic goals here.

Aside from this, Maersk is also a play on shipping and capacity availability, which directly influences/decides on pricing. And there is a bit of new industry delivery coming to market in 2025. The two factors going against the company are actually a re-opening of the Red Sea (which would lower freight prices), and that aforementioned capacity increase. At the same time, any red sea opening would also result in likely port congestion, if only in the short term.

If you think the prospects and potentials sound uncertain, it's because they, in my view, certainly are. The primary way we approach this uncertainty is through valuation.

Some analysts have raised their targets. What about me?

Valuation for Maersk - I believe my price target to be accurate

I don't increase my price targets for short-term catalysts and trends. So, for Maersk to get an increased price target due to what has been happening here would require long-term increased prospects.

Because of the above-mentioned issues with the Red Sea opening and freight price volatility, I don't consider this company to be interesting here, unless bought at a 15% annualized upside to a conservative price target. That conservative price target upside is not something we have here.

My lowering of the price target, or PT, was to a native 11,800 DKK per share. The company is now trading at over 13,500 DKK/share. Maersk is incredibly difficult to forecast accurately, especially in the earnings department. There has been almost a 100% failure to do so over the past 10 years, with the company either massively beating or massively failing to hit those targets and forecasts.

Other analysts who have raised their targets have done so usually on the basis of tariff clarity, and usually using EBITDA as a basis at a 2.5-3x level, with some going to 3.5x, as well as using realized freight rates as a primary KPI. I personally do not believe freight rates without the Red Sea issue will be able to rise above $1,800/share, and because of the Red Sea situation being temporary, I believe it to be only a matter of time before Maersk drops back down to reflect this. What you have to realize is that current freight rates of over or around $2,200 on a realized basis for the company here are inflated, and no amount of savings or efficiencies from the Hub-and-Spoke model can account for an over 20% difference in earnings from the realized freight rates here.

I also question the wisdom of other analysts of increasing, even temporarily, based on a temporary upside, when the company itself has the uncertainty that it has. Maersk is, in my view, not undervalued on the basis of EBIT or EBITDA, and when it comes to the upside from its efficiency and other projects, I'm more of the mindset of "show me."

I therefore view Maersk as being overvalued here to my remaining PT of 11,800 DKK. The only other comparable peer that could be interesting next to this company is Hapag Lloyd, native ticker HLAG. While slightly better valued than Maersk and with a competitive yield, I see much of the same uncertainties here, which lead me to be careful with this company as well.

All in all, I do not think these companies currently make for good investments. In fact, if Maersk was to rise above 14-15k DKK, I might start rotating my position in the company and taking home some of the profit i've made.

Risks to my thesis are as follows.

Risks to my thesis

The risks to my thesis can still be viewed in two ways. Either if the company does significantly better than expected, and my rating results in missing out on further growth and profit (though again, I do have a position in the company, and would see part of this profit).

Then there is the risk of a worse performance than expected if things go south. As mentioned, in the scenario of a significant recession in the U.S., I would cut my target below 10,500 at least to reflect what I believe to be meaningful volume and profit declines, even if the future seems to mean fewer volumes from the U.S. in general. If the conflict situation persists for long in the Middle East, and if the Red Sea situation worsens further, it's also not unrealistic to see a significant medium-term impact - though as we've seen here, some of these effects are in fact positive.

So it's a very fluid situation, which is why I would argue at this valuation and at this macro, Maersk shouldn't necessarily be a focus for you. If we see a PT of below 11,000, that may change, but for now, my PT update is to 11,800 DKK/share, and my rating is hold. For OTCPK:AMKBF, the ADR, this comes to a new PT of ~$1,850, which reiterates and confirms my Hold rating given the current price of over $2,000.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

  • This is one of the world's most important shipping companies. At a good valuation, this company is, to me, a no-nonsense "Buy" with no looking back. The flow of goods dictates the state of our national economies, which makes the company a major player in macroeconomics and trade.
  • I view the company currently as fairly valued, with a long-term upside, but a shorter-term downside due to exposure to the logistics and freight cycle.
  • It's a "Buy" here to me, and I view it with a target of 11,800 DKK/share updated for late June 2025, a cut to reflect the current macro and political situation. I would buy more of the company below 11,000, given that there are still more attractive investment options out there at this time.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them:

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of 11,800 DKK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of August 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: July PCE Data: Unfolding Stagflation Should Worry The Fed
data.description:
July PCE data

July PCE data came as expected:

  • Core PCE inflation increased by 0.3%, which pushed the annual core PCE inflation to 2.9%,
  • Headline PCE inflation increased by 0.2%, which kept the annual PCE inflation at 2.6%,
  • Personal income increased by 0.4% while personal spending
  • Increased by 0.5% in July.

The main takeaway is that the core PCE inflation has increased to 2.9%, and this is the Fed's preferred inflation measure for setting monetary policy - inflation is going the wrong way, and it's inching towards the 3% level, while the target is still at 2%.

The big picture - stagflation

The Fed expects that inflation will rise over the next few months due to the net effects of policies in place (tariffs, immigration) and at the same time growth is expected to slow with a weakening labor market.

However, the Fed's view is that the increase in inflation will be only a one-time effect (gradual) thus the Fed will be willing to look through rising inflation and focus on the weakening labor market. Thus, the Fed is considering cutting interest rates.

The major risk to this dovish outlook is a possible price-wage inflationary spiral, which would force the Fed to turn hawkish and even possibly increase interest rates.

The July PCE data points to rising inflation but not slowing consumption. Thus, on the surface, the July data does not support the stagflationary scenario, only the inflationary scenario.

Inflation and wages

Consumer incomes increased by 0.4% in July, which is considered positive for consumption, with real incomes increasing by 0.2% after adjusting for PCE inflation.

However, the major source of personal income is wage and salary income, which increased by 0.6%, the highest this year. Wage inflation reflects the balance between labor demand and labor supply. If the labor supply is decreasing due to the immigration policy, rising wages reflect an even larger increase in labor demand, which points to a labor shortage.

This is exactly what the Fed does not want to see, rising wages, as this could potentially trigger the price-wage inflationary spiral and make the rising inflation more persistent.

So, July data show that wage income is rising, and this is negative for inflationary expectations.

Consumption and stagflation

Consumer spending increased by $109M in July, and a third of that was spent on motor vehicles and parts. Thus, it appears that consumers are still rushing to beat the expected increase in vehicles and parts due to tariffs, and this could be a problem for consumption in the subsequent months.

Specifically, the recent consumption of vehicles could lead to a significant decrease once the tariffs kick in, and this could lead to a decrease in personal spending and a possible recession. That's the "stag" part of stagflation, as the same effect occurs in all durable goods (and some non-durable) subject to higher tariffs.

The front-loading ahead of tariffs is also evident from the higher-than-expected trade deficit for July, at §103M

Personal consumption expenditures increased by 0.4%, and this was driven by a 1.9% increase in durable goods, and that's essentially the frontloading of consumption, as previously discussed.

However, durable goods prices decreased by -0.1%, which pushed the goods inflation also down by -0.1%. This means that retailers, primarily car dealers, were liquidating inventories at discounts.

Thus, real personal expenditures on non-durable goods increased by 0.9%, led by a 2% increase in durable goods.

Note, the service inflation increased by 0.3% in July, which is the highest since February. So, in July, we observe lower goods inflation, which is likely only temporary before tariffs kick in, and rising service inflation. Both are negative and support the stagflationary scenario. The non-durable goods prices are likely to rise, while service inflation is also rising. Given that income is rising due to higher wages, this supports a negative inflation outlook. Further, consumption is likely to fall after the frontloading consumption fades.

Implications for the Fed

The Fed is looking at the annual PCE inflation numbers, and the July data shows: 1) an increase in service inflation to 3.6%, 2) a decrease in goods inflation to 0.5%, but an increase in durable goods prices to 1.1%.

Again, the trend is worrisome for the Fed; service inflation is not falling, while non-durable goods inflation is creeping higher.

The most worrisome fact is that consumer income is rising primarily due to higher wages. This possibly suggests that there is a labor shortage, which is likely to get even worse as mass deportations progress. Thus, the Fed could be facing a nightmare scenario of slower growth and a persistent increase in inflation due to a labor shortage.

Thus, the July PCE data does not support an interest rate cut in September. In fact, the Fed could turn more hawkish at the September FOMC meeting. Going forward, the August labor market report will be important, especially the wage growth data. If the labor market data support a possibly unfolding price-wage inflationary spiral, the Fed could turn hawkish in September.

Implications for the markets

The bond market has been frozen lately, and this suggests indecisiveness due to uncertainty. The short-term interest rates (US2Y) are still pricing a modest monetary policy easing, with the Federal Funds futures pricing 89% of a September interest rate cut.

The data does not support the September cut, so this could be a major source of volatility. The market is possibly pricing a cut due to the political interference or pressure from the Trump administration, which is gaining more control of the FOMC. However, the long-term interest rates (US10Y) are also frozen waiting for the resolution; if the Fed cuts due to political interference, the yields could spike higher as inflation expectations de-anchor.

The stock market is a bubble - trading at extreme valuations for the S&P 500 (SP500), pricing goldilocks, the Fed cut, and riding the AI bubble. In fact, the market behaved in 1998, expecting the bubble to continue to inflate. But the reality is different. The PCE report shows an unfolding stagflation, which is likely to accelerate, and this suggests an imminent bubble burst.

data.title: EHang Holdings: Banking On Scalable UAM Model For Growth
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I am reiterating my previous bullish rating on EHang Holdings Limited (NASDAQ:EH) driven by its integrated UAM platform. In my last article, I focused on EHang’s eVTOL business model and highlighted the certification successes it has attained from thousands of flight tests to becoming China’s first eVTOL company to receive passenger-carrying certification. Still, EH216-S continues to be EHang’s main revenue driver with its commercial operations in Q2'2025, recording 700 safe flights, delivering 68 units and receiving 150 new orders, which demonstrates strong demand. This highlights the long-term potential of my previous article, and I believe there is yet more upside potential. Having said that, the fact that its core business, which is eVTOL, continues to soar does not negate the fact that there are opportunities in its diversified portfolio. The company has a vision to build smart cities, provide aerial tourism, logistics services, and emergency medical transport services.

For example, let’s look at the vision of building a smart city. The Smart City Expo & Tomorrow Mobility World Congress 2024, held in Barcelona, is an eye-opener for companies pioneering smart cities eVTOL utilities, starting with EH216-S. From a broader perspective, the global eVTOL market is projected to reach $1.5 trillion by 2040, driven by a steady increase in demand for urban applications, including cargo delivery, passenger transport, and aerial imaging, among others. Smart cities will be a major driver of this growth.

From my previous article, you now understand that EHang is in the final stages of mass production for commercialization after the type certificate. From this angle, I am finding out what opportunities EHang sees in smart cities. Given the growth projection of smart cities, which is at a CAGR of 15.5% between 2024 and 2030, it is right to say that EHang is eyeing to seize this opportunity.

Based on my perspective above, I believe the company is creating a wide revenue base from smart cities, which leads me to my explanations below on how I think EHang is positioning itself for the smart cities’ applications.

Smart Cities Are Key To EHang's Growth

I will begin exploring what EHang is looking forward to in its vision of having an integrated vertical UAM platform. In my view, the smart cities market opportunity is the major driver in this diversification. For example, the smart cities market volume is estimated to reach $1.610 trillion by 2030 from $588.4 billion in 2023. This is expected to grow at a CAGR of 15.5% between 2024 and 2025.

One of the major drivers of smart cities is the growing concern for sustainability, particularly in how cities can reduce their environmental footprint. For instance, half of the global population resides in cities and is responsible for 70% of the global greenhouse gases. Furthermore, investors continue to show interest in smart cities, with current investment of around $1 billion. This indicates there is a commitment to the sector’s innovation and growth. So far, there are over 1,003 smart city startups, which means that the road to autonomous mobility is also inevitable. From a global perspective, South Korea and Singapore lead in the implementation of smart cities; however, the United States is expected to generate the most revenues from the smart city market in 2025, at approximately $27.06 billion. This hints at the current state of the smart city market and the future growth opportunity.

EHang is currently entering into partnerships to facilitate market entry into emerging smart cities, such as EHang’s debut at the Smart City Expo World Congress in Spain. This expo included cooperation with Vertiports Network, a European Advanced Air Mobility (AAM). Since the expo included governments, enterprises, and research and innovation firms, it is right to say that EHang is at the forefront of planning urban transportation and smart cities.

More cooperation has been made, including EHang’s partnership with Reignwood Aviation to deploy EH215-S. This partnership is facilitating low-altitude tourism, but the two companies plan to expand the service to aerial logistics, passenger transportation and emergency response. Additionally, EHang has an agreement with the Anshun Government, which has resulted in a purchase order of 50 units of EH216-S autonomous eVTOLs. EHang has also entered into a RMB 1 billion partnership with the Hefei Government for the establishment of the new VT35 series, which is also autonomous, long-range, and offering lift and cruise services.

Upon examining these agreements, it becomes clear that EHang is preparing to lead the smart low-altitude city’s low-altitude mobility and other applications. For instance, when I examine Abu Dhabi’s smart city plan, the city is ambitious to transform its urban mobility into smart urban mobility, utilizing eVTOL air taxis and other services such as transportation. In these plans, EHang is involved in Abu Dhabi’s smart urban mobility. EHang has confirmed that it is planning to establish its regional presence in this city, including a final assembly line and other support services. So far, EHang has conducted a flagship of its autonomous eVTOL EH216-S. This flagship, a collaboration between EHang and Multi Level Group (MLG), introduces a new sky taxi mode and other means of cargo transportation through low-altitude transportation, typically renowned as the next-generation transportation.

Considering smart city growth as the major driver to an integrated UAM platform, partnerships are the first step EHang is taking as an entry mechanism using its EH216-S series. The EH216-S series is pilotless, and this autonomy means that it is a smart eVTOL ready for smart cities. Therefore, instead of using EH216-S in logistics, EH17-L is using EH217-L for the logistics ecosystem, which has a maximum payload of 250 kg. This eVTOL utilizes cloud computing and autonomy, with no need for manual intervention.

In my view, EHang is gradually preparing to enter the smart city ecosystem through partnerships that broaden its revenue base from passenger eVTOLs to an integrated UAM platform offering logistics, smart city management and aerial media. As the first eVTOL to receive complete suite certifications, I believe EHang will capture a bigger share of smart city urban mobility revenue from the overall smart city revenue of $1.380 trillion by 2030.

EH Stock - Financials And Valuation

The company is performing well in terms of revenue with Q2'2025 recording 44.2% YoY to RMB147.2 million ($20.5 million). This represented a significant improvement from its previous quarter’s revenue of RMB 26.1 million ($3.6 million), reflecting an upside of 464%. This was primarily driven by the unit’s delivery, which increased from 11 units in the first quarter of 2025 to 68 units of the EH216 series in Q2’2025. From an annual perspective, its revenue has been on solid upward momentum over the last couple of years, turning a -68.46% YoY growth in 2021 into a consistent revenue improvement in the subsequent years culminating in a 288.46% YoY growth in 2024. Although the growth could normalize due to the normal growth curve, I expect it to remain strong backed by the reasons I discussed above.

You may wonder why I am bullish, despite the company having reduced its revenue guidance from RMB900 to RMB500. I do not see this compressed guidance as a reason to worry because, from my analysis, the company is building partnerships to scale the demand for its eVTOLs. This aligns well with the company’s rationale, which is to establish a solid foundation for its products as the first mover to capture a larger market share in terms of product manufacturer and as a service provider. The company is still conducting flight tests in Hefei, and this gives me a reason to understand why, despite entering into these partnerships, it first needs to perform flight tests before mass production and commercialization. I am maintaining a long-term outlook because the company is focusing on building a broad revenue base from its integrated UAM platform rather than chasing short-term hype. The compressed guidance is an intentional decision for EHang as it seeks to prioritize modernizing its 2025 delivery to more scalable, safe and regular commercial eVTOL operations.

Nevertheless, the company still maintains a strong gross margin of 62.5%, a slight improvement from its first quarter of 2025, at 62.4% which means it is retaining a larger portion of its revenues after paying its operating costs. This is a sign of operational discipline and efficiency.

In terms of profitability, EHang remains a loss-making entity, with Q2 2025 recording a net loss of RMB 81.0 million ($11.3 million), a 13.1% increase from RMB 78.4 million ($10.8 million). This increase in net loss was driven by a non-operating expense of RMB13.7 million ($1.9 million) for the 2023 US securities class action. This is a short-term hiccup that does not impact the long-term development, and I angle and reiterate my bullish outlook. Overall, in terms of annual profitability, the net loss is declining steadily as the company approaches mass production and initiates partnerships, except for the TTM loss, which has gone up as explained previously.

Most importantly, considering the company's trailing total operating expenses of $78.9 million and its current cash and short-term investments of $156.32 million, it has a cash runway of 1.98, which is approximately 2 years. I consider this a strong liquidity position as it shows the sustainability of the company since it's a loss-making firm.

Again, looking at the trailing revenue growth of about 87% and the last two years' revenue growth in the north of 100%, I am seeing normalizing revenue growth and as such, I will assume an average annual revenue growth rate of 60% between 2025 and 2027 to estimate the company's financial performance. Given these assumptions, I project a revenue of about $256 million by 2027, which is in close proximity to consensus estimates of $236 million in 2027.

Looking at profitability, the company has made significant strides with its net margin improving from about -750% in 2023 to its current level of -54.45% which marks an improvement of more than 92% hinting at profitability soon.

While I don't expect this high growth rate to be sustained, I will assume a growth rate of about 20% in 2026 and 2027 because the curve seems to be flattening which is normal towards profitability as opposed to when losses were high and costs were spread on a small revenue base. Given this assumption, I estimate a net income margin of 10.89% in 2026 and 13.07% in 2027, implying that EH is likely to break even next year.

Moving to valuation, I will compare EHang’s valuation against that of its competitors, Cadre Holdings, Inc. (CDRE) and V2X, Inc. (VVX), using the P/S metric. The EHang P/S metric suggests that it is trading at a premium given its P/S of 17.37x compared to its peers with CDRE at a P/S ratio of 2.20x and VVX at a P/S ratio of 0.43x.

This may suggest that EHang is overvalued. However, I think that this is justified given its high trailing revenue growth YoY at 84.04% which is significantly high compared to its competitors, whose revenue growth YoY ranges below 8%. Given the company catalysts I have discussed above which allude to a bright revenue outlook and owing to its trailing growth relative to its peers, I think its premium valuation is justified.

Besides its growth which warrants its premium pricing, I also find the company better positioned than its peers due to several reasons. I give two examples. First off, EH is a pure play autonomous aerial vehicle company as opposed to CDRE and VVX, who are traditional defense and tactical solutions thus lacking the exposure to the fast-growing UAM at a CAGR of 35% between 2024 and 2030.

Secondly, EH has a leaner operational structure with less than 500(483) employees as opposed to VVX's more than 16,000 and CDRE's more than 2,000 staff respectively. This lean setup grants them lower operational costs which leads to strong gross profit relative to its peers. This in my view has the potential for shorter innovation cycles both from the cost perspective and with fewer complexities in larger operational setups.

To me this is a major advantage in emerging tech sectors as it is likely to lead to new innovations with ease thus the possibility of staying ahead of the curve. In my view, this also explains its first-mover advantage in the industry.

Investment risks

Legal exposure – the company is facing a U.S. Securities Class Action case filed in 2023, which is draining its cash reserves through settlement fees used for the same purpose.

Regulatory risks – The company is taking the time to conduct flight tests with its partners before proceeding to full-scale production. This is delaying its revenues.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my previous bullish rating in this article. I believe that the integrated UAM platform positions EHang for increased revenue generation as it plans to capture a larger share by facilitating smart city next-generation transportation.

data.title: AbbVie Smashes Earnings, Bets Big On Depression Drug Bretisilocin
data.description:
Since my last article, "Why AbbVie's Stock Is A Strong Buy After Q1 2025 Results," AbbVie's stock price (NYSE:ABBV) (NEOE:ABBV:CA) has risen 11.5%.

It currently trades near its 52-week high of $209.5, thanks in part to strong sales of Skyrizi and Rinvoq, which were key contributors to beating Wall Street's consensus revenue estimate by $392.7 million.

In this article, I will provide an analysis of AbbVie's acquisition of Gilgamesh Pharmaceuticals' bretisilocin and its financial results for Q2 2025.

AbbVie made a smart move by acquiring Gilgamesh's bretisilocin

Before I discuss the deal itself, I want to go back a bit and remind you how the relationship between these two companies began.

On May 13, 2024, AbbVie entered into a partnership agreement with Gilgamesh Pharmaceuticals worth up to $2.06 billion to develop next-generation neuroplastogens to develop next-generation neuroplastogens to treat people with a variety of mental disorders.

And now, just over a year later, on August 25, 2025, AbbVie announced the acquisition of Gilgamesh's bretisilocin for up to $1.2 billion.

You, like me, may have a few questions.

Why does AbbVie need bretisilocin?

Overall, AbbVie has a strong position in the CNS therapeutics market thanks to Vraylar, Botox, Ubrelvy, Vyalev, and Qulipta. And its former CEO Richard Gonzalez and current CEO Robert Michael are working to reduce AbbVie's financial dependence on sales of its immunology franchise.

However, the failure of its two Phase 2 EMPOWER studies evaluating emraclidine for schizophrenia, the success of this strategy was somewhat shaky, and, in my view, what was needed was a product candidate that combined a promising mechanism of action and already demonstrated extremely encouraging efficacy in the treatment of a widespread disease.

And as a result, this experimental drug became bretisilocin.

What is the mechanism of action of bretisilocin?

It is a 5-HT2A receptor agonist and 5-HT releaser.

In simple terms, bretisilocin activates a receptor called 5-HT2A, which leads to an acceleration of the formation of connections between neurons and thus helps a patient with depression to reduce the number of negative thoughts circulating in his head and also to look at his problems/experiences from a different angle.

Plus, unlike such widely used antidepressants as Eli Lilly's Prozac (LLY) and Pfizer's Zoloft (PFE), the mechanism of action of which is aimed at inhibiting the reuptake of the well-known hormone serotonin [5-HT], bretisilocin, on the contrary, causes its release, thereby acting faster in transmitting signals directly related to motivation or a sense of satisfaction with life.

Thus, I make the first conclusion, and it is that, only taking into account the mechanism of action of bretisilocin, it represents a promising product candidate for the treatment of moderate-to-severe MDD.

Were the results of the Phase 2a clinical trial strong?

On May 27, Gilgamesh Pharmaceuticals published the results of a Phase 2a study involving 40 people suffering from moderate-to-severe major depressive disorder.

According to the data, taking bretisilocin led to a statistically significant decrease in the MADRS [Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale] compared to those in the low-dose psychoactive comparator group.

In addition, it demonstrated a faster antidepressant effect in the first 24 hours, and in my opinion, what is more important and clearly shows the efficacy of bretisilocin [GM-2505, 10 mg] is that 70% of patients achieved MADRS remission by day 14 from the start of treatment compared to 25% of those in the comparison group.

Also, Gilgamesh stated in its press release that the safety profile of bretisilocin was promising, with no serious adverse events.

Overall, I analyzed the clinical data of Axsome's Auvelity (AXSM) and Johnson & Johnson's Caplyta (JNJ), and I can say that AbbVie was not mistaken in purchasing bretisilocin in my view, which demonstrated higher efficacy in the fight against this common mood disorder, which, according to NIH, affects 21 million Americans.

AbbVie's revenue continues to grow amid strong sales from Skyrizi and Rinvoq

So, AbbVie's revenue was $15.42 billion in the second quarter of 2025, beating the consensus estimate by $392.7 million.

It increased 6.6% year-over-year and 15.6% quarter-over-quarter, which to me is a clear indication that declining Humira sales [$1.18 billion in Q2 2025 vs. $2.81 billion in Q2 2024] and the weak performance of its Eye Care franchise are no longer having a significant impact on AbbVie's total revenue.

Its non-GAAP EPS also did not disappoint, reaching $2.97, up 20.7% quarter-over-quarter, and beating my expectations by 5 cents.

At the same time, AbbVie's growing R&D expenses due to the acquisitions of Capstan Therapeutics, Nimble Therapeutics, and Aliada Therapeutics, as well as the concluded collaboration agreements that I discussed in the article "Why AbbVie Remains A Strong Buy For Long-Term Investors," did not have the negative impact I had previously factored into my model.

So why did its revenue rise so sharply?

In the table below, I have marked the sales of AbbVie's key medications.

What can be understood from this table? What conclusions do I draw?

First, competition from medications like Johnson & Johnson's Stelara, Eli Lilly's Taltz, Bristol Myers Squibb's Sotyktu (BMY), and Novartis' Cosentyx (NVS) also can't slow down the growth of AbbVie's immunology franchise revenue, which amounted to about $7.63 billion in Q2 2025, up 21.8% quarter-on-quarter.

Sales of its superstar Skyrizi [risankizumab] reached $4.42 billion, beating my expectations by $220 million and also up 62.2% from Q2 2024 due to increased demand for it to treat people with psoriatic arthritis and ulcerative colitis.

Meanwhile, sales of AbbVie's second and equally important medication, Rinvoq [upadacitinib], were about $2.03 billion in the second quarter of 2025, up 41.8% year-on-year and 18% quarter-on-quarter.

Now it's time to discuss the performance of its oncology franchise.

So, its net revenues were $1.68 billion for the three months ended June 30, 2025, thus continuing to tread water in the last 4 quarters, which, in my opinion, is not surprising.

Why?

I believe the dark spot of this franchise is Imbruvica [ibrutinib], whose sales reached $754 million, down 9.5% from Q2 2024.

It is, as I mentioned in the article "Top 4 Immunology Stocks Poised For Growth In 2025", an inhibitor of Bruton's tyrosine kinase and has lower efficacy than AstraZeneca's Calquence (AZN) and BeOne Medicines' Brukinsa (ONC).

On the other hand, Elahere [mirvetuximab soravtansine] sales were $159 million in Q2 2025, beating my expectations by $14 million thanks to its accelerated adoption into clinical practice for the treatment of certain patients with ovarian cancer.

How did AbbVie's margins change?

Its gross profit and gross profit margin were about $11.08 billion and 71.8% for the three months ended June 30, 2025, up $817 million and 0.9%, respectively, year-on-year.

AbbVie's operating income margin was 37.5%, up 6.2% quarter-on-quarter, driven by strong performance from its immunology franchise, which I discussed in detail earlier, as well as increased sales of Ubrelvy and Qulipta, approved for the treatment of people with migraine.

At the same time, I would like to point out that it exceeds the operating income margins of its key competitors in the anti-inflammatory and CNS therapeutics markets, such as Johnson & Johnson [28.9%], Pfizer [28.7%], and Sanofi [18%] (SNY).

Has AbbVie made progress in reducing its total debt/EBITDA ratio?

I believe the importance of this question remains high as AbbVie remains active in the M&A space, particularly in pursuit of targets that allow it to strengthen its position in the cancer or mental health markets.

Overall, I wrote in detail about the partnerships and acquisitions that AbbVie has made since 2019 in the article "Why AbbVie Remains A Strong Buy For Long-Term Investors".

AbbVie's total debt was about $70.6 billion at the end of June 2025, down $353 million year-on-year at a rate of 22.3%.

Yes, this number may scare you a little, but I believe it is more important to look at its total debt/EBITDA ratio.

It was 2.51x and continues to move towards the 2x mark, mainly due to AbbVie's growing EBITDA [$7.84 billion in Q2 2025 vs. $7.13 billion in Q2 2024]. At the same time, its credit ratings from S&P Global Ratings [A-, stable outlook] (SPGI) and Moody's Investors Service [A3] (MCO) are at relatively high investment grades.

Did AbbVie use its share repurchase program in Q2?

Unlike the first quarter, when AbbVie bought back its shares for about $961 million, in Q2 2025, its CEO, Robert Michael, decided not to use its share repurchase program.

In my opinion, this decision is quite justified given the acquisition of Nimble Therapeutics and Capstan Therapeutics [up to $2.1 billion in cash], as well as the fact that its short interest remains at an extremely low level according to data provided by Fintel.

Risks

I have updated the table with risks to include my concerns about declining sales of Imbruvica, Duodopa, Alphagan/Combigan, and Trump's ongoing threats and loud statements against Big Pharma.

Takeaway

So, in conclusion, I want to mention again that AbbVie continues to do what it does best, which is succeed in commercializing its blockbusters, including Skyrizi, Rinvoq, Ubrelvy, Epkinly, and Vyalev.

In my opinion, their strong performance, especially over the last year and a half, has allowed AbbVie to remain active in the M&A space with the ultimate goals of reducing its free cash flow [FCF] dependence on sales of the immunology franchise and strengthening its position in the mood disorder treatment market.

In addition, in part because it raised its full-year 2025 adjusted diluted EPS guidance from $11.67-$11.87 to $11.88-$12.08, implying 18.4% year-over-year growth, I continue to cover AbbVie with a 'Strong Buy' rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
